heroOverview: Pleurisy is inflammation of the pleura — the two-layered membrane that lines the lungs and inner chest wall — producing a sharp, stabbing chest pain that worsens sharply with each breath, cough, or movement. It affects an estimated 1 in 1,000 adults each year in high-income countries, and viral respiratory infections account for roughly 40% of all community-acquired cases. The hallmark on examination is a pleural friction rub: a leathery, scratching sound heard through a stethoscope as inflamed pleural surfaces rub against each other. Most acute viral cases resolve within 1-2 weeks without lasting damage, but pleurisy is clinically important because it is frequently a sign of an underlying condition — pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, autoimmune disease, or malignancy — that itself requires targeted treatment. Identifying and treating the cause rather than the chest pain alone is the central principle of management.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 1,000 adults per year in developed countries; more common in winter respiratory illness season
keyStats.demographics: Any age; viral pleurisy peaks in younger adults aged 20-40; autoimmune pleurisy is more common in women of childbearing age
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak; bimodal pattern with viral causes prevalent in young adults and malignant/autoimmune causes in adults over 50
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global incidence not well characterised; pleurisy as a complication of tuberculosis affects millions in endemic regions

definition: Pleurisy (ICD-10: R09.1), also termed pleuritis, is inflammation of the parietal and visceral pleural layers that surround the lungs and line the chest cavity. The pleura consists of two continuous sheets separated by a thin film of lubricating fluid; when inflamed, the surfaces become roughened and adherent, generating pain with respiratory movement. It falls under the care of general medicine and pulmonology, with specialist input from cardiology, rheumatology, or oncology depending on the cause. The condition may occur with or without a pleural effusion — an excess accumulation of fluid between the pleural layers. When fluid is present, it can initially relieve pain by separating the inflamed surfaces, but large effusions impair lung expansion and cause breathlessness. Causes are broadly classified as infectious, autoimmune, malignant, vascular, and drug-related. Viral pleuritis following respiratory tract infections is the most common form in otherwise healthy adults. Bacterial pleuritis typically accompanies pneumonia (parapneumonic pleuritis). Autoimmune pleurisy occurs in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), rheumatoid arthritis, and other connective tissue diseases. Pulmonary embolism causes pleuritis by infarction of peripheral lung, which inflames the adjacent pleura. Malignant mesothelioma and metastatic cancer are important causes in older adults. Uraemic pleuritis complicates advanced kidney failure.

typesClassification.description: The most common type in young, otherwise healthy adults; caused by enteroviruses, influenza, and other respiratory viruses. It resolves spontaneously within 1-2 weeks in most cases with no lasting deficit.
Occurs alongside bacterial pneumonia as inflammation spreads to the adjacent pleura; may progress to an exudative effusion or, if bacteria invade the pleural space, to empyema requiring drainage.
Associated with SLE (pleuritis is a diagnostic criterion in SLE), rheumatoid arthritis, and other connective tissue diseases; often recurs and requires treatment of the underlying condition.

typesClassification.type: Pleuritic chest pain from pulmonary embolism
typesClassification.description: Peripheral emboli cause lung infarction that inflames the adjacent pleura; distinguishing this from benign pleurisy is critical because it requires anticoagulation.
Mesothelioma and metastatic cancer (lung, breast, lymphoma) seed the pleura, causing inflammation, persistent pain, and typically a large exudative effusion that reaccumulates after drainage.
An immune-mediated reaction to pleural infection with Mycobacterium tuberculosis; common in TB-endemic regions and associated with a lymphocyte-rich exudate and sometimes subsequent pleural fibrosis.

primarySymptoms: Sharp, stabbing or knife-like chest pain on one side that worsens acutely with every breath in, cough, sneeze, or movement — the defining symptom that distinguishes pleuritic from other chest pain.
Pain relief when the patient holds the breath, leans toward the affected side, or splints the chest by pressing on it — characteristic of pleuritic pain and helps clinicians identify the source.
Shortness of breath — caused by the patient taking deliberately shallow breaths to avoid pain, and, when a large pleural effusion is present, by mechanical compression of the underlying lung.
A dry, irritating cough that worsens the pain, often without producing sputum in viral cases.
Fever, malaise, and myalgia, which in viral pleurisy accompany the systemic features of the underlying respiratory infection.
Shoulder or neck pain on the affected side, caused by diaphragmatic pleuritis irritating the phrenic nerve (referred pain to the C4 dermatome).
Rapid, shallow breathing pattern as the patient unconsciously minimises chest expansion to limit pain.
A pleural friction rub heard on auscultation — a creaking or leathery sound best heard at end-inspiration and early expiration, disappearing when a large effusion separates the pleural surfaces.
Tracheal deviation away from the affected side if a large effusion compresses the mediastinum.
Reduced breath sounds and dullness to percussion over a co-existing pleural effusion.
earlyWarningSigns: A new aching or catching sensation in the side of the chest that is more pronounced on deep breathing, even before frank sharp pain develops.
Mild breathlessness with a recent respiratory illness that is disproportionate to fever or systemic symptoms.
Finding yourself automatically taking shallow breaths to avoid discomfort in one area of the chest.
A new dry cough following a cold or viral illness, accompanied by any chest discomfort.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe chest pain with breathlessness, rapid heart rate, or low blood pressure — which can indicate pulmonary embolism, pneumothorax, or cardiac tamponade rather than simple pleurisy.
SpO2 below 94% or respiratory rate above 30 breaths per minute.
High fever (above 39°C) with productive cough and pleuritic pain, suggesting an evolving pneumonia with risk of empyema.

Pleuritic pain in the context of recent travel, prolonged immobility, pregnancy, or oral contraceptive use — these raise the probability of pulmonary embolism to a level requiring urgent investigation.
Haemoptysis (coughing up blood) with pleuritic chest pain, which can indicate pulmonary infarction or malignancy.

causes.description: Enteroviruses (particularly Coxsackie B), influenza, adenoviruses, and other respiratory viruses directly infect pleural tissue or trigger an inflammatory response in the adjacent pleura; this produces the majority of community-acquired pleurisy in healthy adults and typically resolves without specific treatment within two weeks.

Bacterial infection of the lung parenchyma spreads inflammation to the visceral pleura; Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus, and Gram-negative organisms are the most common culprits. If bacteria invade the pleural space, empyema develops and requires drainage.

A peripheral pulmonary embolus causes downstream lung infarction, with necrosis of the lung tissue adjacent to the pleura triggering intense pleuritis; this is the most immediately dangerous cause of pleuritic chest pain because it requires anticoagulation therapy to prevent further emboli.

SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, and other connective tissue disorders trigger immune-complex deposition or T-cell-mediated inflammation in the pleura; in SLE, pleuritis is one of the 11 diagnostic criteria and occurs in up to 60% of patients during the disease course.

Primary pleural tumours (mesothelioma, strongly linked to asbestos exposure) and metastatic cancer from the lung, breast, ovary, or lymphoma seed the pleural surfaces, producing persistent inflammation, pain, and often a large exudative effusion that reaccumulates rapidly after drainage.

An immune-mediated pleural reaction to Mycobacterium tuberculosis produces a lymphocyte-rich exudative effusion and pleuritis that can arise even without radiologically visible pulmonary TB; it is a leading cause of pleurisy in TB-endemic regions and requires full anti-tuberculous therapy.
Accumulation of uraemic toxins in kidney failure produces a fibrinous, painful pleuritis and pericarditis; it resolves with adequate dialysis but recurs if dialysis is inadequate.

riskFactors.description: The most common trigger; viral pleurisy follows influenza, Coxsackie B infection, and other respiratory illnesses, with the inflammatory response reaching the pleura within days of the primary infection.
Pleuritis is a recognised feature of SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, and other connective tissue disorders; roughly 30-60% of SLE patients develop pleuritis at some point during their illness.
Prolonged immobility, oral contraceptive use, pregnancy, recent surgery, active cancer, and thrombophilia increase the risk of PE-related pleuritis, the most dangerous cause.
Living in or travelling to TB-endemic areas significantly raises the probability that an unexplained lymphocytic pleural effusion with pleurisy represents tuberculous pleuritis.
Occupational or environmental asbestos exposure raises the risk of mesothelioma, which causes chronic pleuritis; the latency period between exposure and disease is typically 20-50 years.
Immunosuppressive medications and conditions impair the ability to contain viral and bacterial pleural infections, increasing severity and the risk of progression to empyema.
Uraemic pleuritis complicates end-stage renal disease; adequate dialysis is preventive and therapeutic.

affectedDemographics: Young healthy adults aged 20-40 with viral pleurisy following respiratory illnesses
Women of childbearing age with autoimmune conditions such as SLE
Adults over 50 with malignancy-related or post-pneumonic pleuritis
People in TB-endemic regions or with recent TB exposure
Workers with historical asbestos exposure (for mesothelioma-related pleurisy)
Hospitalised patients, post-surgical patients, and those with recent prolonged immobility (PE-related pleurisy)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with the clinical history — the character of pleuritic pain (sharp, lateralised, worse with breathing), its onset, and associated features — and a physical examination that may reveal a pleural friction rub. In a young, otherwise healthy adult with a recent viral illness, the combination of typical pleuritic pain and a rub supports a clinical diagnosis of viral pleurisy, and only minimal investigation is needed initially. However, because dangerous causes including pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, and malignancy must be actively excluded, a structured approach is standard. Blood tests include a full blood count, inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR), and D-dimer when pulmonary embolism is clinically possible (a D-dimer below the age-adjusted threshold reliably excludes PE in low-to-intermediate probability patients). Chest X-ray is the first imaging step, showing pleural effusion, pneumonia, or pneumothorax. CT pulmonary angiography is the gold standard for pulmonary embolism. CT of the chest with contrast characterises pleural thickening, masses, and loculated effusions. Pleural ultrasound detects and guides drainage of effusions. When an effusion is present, diagnostic thoracentesis provides fluid for protein (to classify as exudate or transudate by Light's criteria), LDH, glucose, pH, cytology, Gram stain, and culture. Autoimmune workup (ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, RF) is performed when the history suggests a connective tissue disease. Pleural biopsy is reserved for unexplained exudates where malignancy or TB is suspected after initial testing. Echocardiography excludes pericarditis, which can mimic pleurisy closely.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to detect pleural effusion, pneumonia, pneumothorax, or cardiomegaly and to guide the differential diagnosis of pleuritic chest pain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You stand in front of an X-ray panel and hold a brief breath; the scan takes seconds and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard test to exclude pulmonary embolism as a cause of pleuritic chest pain, and to characterise pleural and lung abnormalities in detail.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast dye is injected into a vein, and you pass through a CT scanner for a few minutes; you may feel a brief warm sensation from the contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and quantifies pleural effusion, guides safe drainage, and identifies features suggesting exudate, empyema, or malignancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel-covered probe is pressed against the side of the chest; the procedure is painless and takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Samples pleural fluid to classify it as exudate or transudate, and to test for infection, malignant cells, tuberculosis, and other specific causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anaesthetic is injected into the back of the chest wall, then a needle or small catheter is inserted to drain fluid; the procedure takes 15-30 minutes and causes mild discomfort at the injection site.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (FBC, CRP, D-dimer, ANA, RF)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for infection and inflammation (FBC, CRP), excludes pulmonary embolism in appropriate patients (D-dimer), and tests for autoimmune causes (ANA, RF).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm with results typically available within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes pericarditis and pericardial effusion, which can present with similar chest pain, and detects cardiac causes of pleural effusion such as heart failure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel-covered ultrasound probe is moved over the chest for 15-30 minutes; painless with no radiation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment targets both the symptom of pleuritic pain and the underlying cause, which must be identified and treated specifically. For viral pleurisy — the most common form — the mainstay is analgesia. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, indomethacin) are first-line for pain relief and also reduce pleural inflammation, providing relief in 70-80% of cases; indomethacin at 25-50 mg three times a day is often preferred for its anti-pleuritic potency. Colchicine, which is established in recurrent pericarditis, is used for recurrent viral pleurisy at 0.5 mg twice daily and has been shown in observational studies to reduce recurrence. Paracetamol can be added for additional analgesia. Opioids are occasionally needed for severe pain but suppress the cough reflex and respiratory effort, so they are used sparingly. Corticosteroids are reserved for autoimmune pleurisy that does not respond to NSAIDs; their use in viral pleurisy may promote recurrence. Treating the underlying cause is paramount: antibiotics for bacterial pneumonia and evolving empyema; anticoagulation (low molecular weight heparin followed by direct oral anticoagulants) for PE; immunosuppressants for autoimmune pleurisy; anti-TB therapy for tuberculous pleuritis; and oncological management for malignant pleurisy. Large or symptomatic effusions are drained via thoracentesis or intercostal drain. Empyema requires prompt drainage — by intercostal tube with or without intrapleural fibrinolytics — and appropriate antibiotics, and sometimes surgical decortication for complex loculated empyema. Malignant effusions that repeatedly reaccumulate are managed with pleurodesis (chemical scarring of the pleural space) or a long-term indwelling pleural catheter.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, indomethacin)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesic and anti-inflammatory treatment for pleuritic pain; indomethacin has the strongest anti-pleuritic evidence and is often given at 25-50 mg three times daily with food. Taken for 1-4 weeks depending on the course.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide clinically meaningful pain relief in approximately 70-80% of viral pleurisy cases within 24-48 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Colchicine
medicalTreatments.description: An anti-inflammatory agent used as an adjunct or alternative to NSAIDs for recurrent or prolonged pleurisy; typically 0.5 mg twice daily for 3-6 months. Well-established in pericarditis and increasingly used for recurrent pleurisy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In pericarditis with pleuritis (peri-myocarditis) trials, colchicine reduced recurrence at 18 months by approximately 50% versus NSAIDs alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics (for bacterial/empyema pleurisy)
medicalTreatments.description: Culture-directed antibiotics targeting the causative organism are required for parapneumonic effusion and empyema; broad-spectrum cover (e.g. co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam for hospital-acquired cases) is started empirically and narrowed by culture results.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure of empyema depends on both adequate antibiotics and drainage; medical management alone of early non-loculated parapneumonic effusions achieves resolution in approximately 70-80% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticoagulation (for PE-related pleurisy)
medicalTreatments.description: When pulmonary embolism is confirmed as the cause, therapeutic anticoagulation with LMWH and direct oral anticoagulants such as rivaroxaban or apixaban stops further thrombosis and allows the embolus to be dissolved by the body's fibrinolytic system.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Anticoagulation reduces the risk of fatal recurrent PE by approximately 90% in confirmed cases according to major RCT data.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (for autoimmune pleurisy)
medicalTreatments.description: Used when NSAID-resistant pleurisy is driven by SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, or other inflammatory conditions; typically prednisolone 20-40 mg daily, tapered over several weeks alongside disease-modifying therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide rapid symptom relief in the majority of autoimmune pleuritis cases; long-term benefit requires treatment of the underlying autoimmune condition.

surgicalOptions.name: Intercostal drain (chest tube) with fibrinolytics
surgicalOptions.description: A flexible tube is inserted between the ribs into the pleural space to drain empyema or large effusions; intrapleural fibrinolytics (streptokinase, tPA) are instilled to break up loculations and improve drainage in complicated parapneumonic effusions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful resolution of empyema with tube drainage plus fibrinolytics in approximately 70-80% of cases, reducing the need for surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) decortication
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive surgery removes the thick fibrous rind covering the lung in organised empyema and frees the trapped, unexpanded lung; preferred over open thoracotomy for most cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Lung re-expansion and symptom resolution achieved in approximately 85-95% of appropriate empyema cases, with lower morbidity than open surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Pleurodesis and indwelling pleural catheter
surgicalOptions.description: For recurrent malignant effusions, chemical agents (talc, doxycycline) are instilled to scar the pleural space shut (pleurodesis), or a long-term drainage catheter is inserted for repeated at-home drainage, avoiding hospital admissions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Talc pleurodesis controls malignant effusion recurrence in approximately 80-90% of appropriately selected patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Heat application and position adjustment
alternativeTreatments.description: Applying a warm compress to the affected side and lying on the painful side (splinting) can modestly reduce pleuritic pain by reducing chest wall movement; useful as an adjunct, not a substitute for analgesia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Breathing exercises post-resolution
alternativeTreatments.description: Once acute pain settles, deep breathing exercises and incentive spirometry help re-expand areas of the lung that were underventilated during the acute episode, preventing atelectasis and facilitating recovery.

whySeeSpecialist: A pulmonologist should be involved when pleurisy does not resolve within 2-3 weeks, when a pleural effusion requires sampling or drainage, when an autoimmune or malignant cause is suspected, or when empyema is a possibility. Cardiology input is needed if pericarditis is suspected. Rheumatology involvement is appropriate when autoimmune pleurisy is part of a systemic connective tissue disease requiring long-term disease-modifying treatment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a pulmonologist or chest physician with access to pleural ultrasound, CT-guided procedures, and a thoracic surgery team for empyema management. For autoimmune pleurisy, a rheumatologist experienced with SLE or rheumatoid arthritis who can co-manage treatment is essential. If mesothelioma is suspected, a centre specialising in thoracic oncology and able to offer early surgical assessment provides the best outcomes.
preventionStrategies: Annual influenza vaccination reduces the risk of viral pleurisy following influenza infection.
Manage autoimmune conditions proactively with disease-modifying agents to reduce the frequency of flares with pleuritis.
Reduce pulmonary embolism risk by mobilising early after surgery, using prophylactic anticoagulation when indicated, and wearing compression stockings on long flights.
Seek prompt treatment for pneumonia to prevent progression to parapneumonic effusion or empyema.
Avoid asbestos exposure through safe work practices; there is no safe level of asbestos for mesothelioma prevention.
lifestyleModifications: Rest and reduce physical activity during the acute phase to minimise chest wall movement and pain.
Use a firm pillow or cushion pressed against the painful side when coughing to reduce the pain of coughing.
Sleep in a position that minimises chest movement on the affected side; many patients find lying on the painful side reduces movement and pain.
Attend follow-up appointments to confirm resolution, particularly if an effusion was present, since slow reaccumulation can be missed.
Avoid smoking, which impairs mucociliary clearance and increases susceptibility to the respiratory infections that trigger pleurisy.

recommended: Adequate fluid intake to keep secretions moist and easy to clear
High-protein, nutrient-dense foods to support immune recovery from the underlying infection
Anti-inflammatory foods — oily fish, olive oil, berries, and leafy greens — as a supportive dietary pattern, though not evidence-based for pleurisy specifically
avoid: NSAIDs on an empty stomach — always take with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects
Alcohol while taking colchicine or high-dose NSAIDs, which increases gastric and hepatic side effects
Smoking, which worsens respiratory infections and delays recovery

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity should be reduced during the acute phase while pleuritic pain is significant. Deep breathing exercises — gentle, progressive breath holds to full inspiration — should be started as soon as pain allows, to prevent the lung collapse (atelectasis) that can follow splinting. Return to normal activity is guided by pain resolution, typically within 1-3 weeks for viral pleurisy. Sports and vigorous exercise can resume when full deep breathing is comfortable without pain.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed NSAIDs or colchicine regularly at the recommended interval rather than only when pain peaks, to maintain steady anti-inflammatory levels.
Note whether pain is improving, staying the same, or worsening day by day — failure to improve after one week warrants review.
Report new fever above 38.5°C, increasing breathlessness, or a change in the character of the pain, which may indicate a developing empyema or change in diagnosis.
Perform 5-10 slow deep breaths every hour during waking hours once the acute pain has settled, to keep the lung bases expanded.
Attend scheduled follow-up imaging to confirm effusion resolution and rule out underlying disease.
prognosis: Viral pleurisy resolves fully in the great majority of patients within 1-4 weeks without long-term sequelae. Approximately 15-30% of patients develop recurrent episodes, which tend to be milder and also self-limited. Parapneumonic pleurisy treated promptly carries an excellent prognosis; empyema without timely drainage can lead to fibrothorax (permanent fibrous trapping of the lung) in a minority of patients. Autoimmune pleurisy recurs according to the course of the underlying disease and requires sustained disease management. Malignant pleurisy carries a prognosis determined by the primary cancer; median survival from the time of malignant effusion diagnosis is 3-12 months depending on cancer type. Tuberculous pleurisy responds well to standard 6-month anti-TB therapy in drug-susceptible disease, and pleural calcification visible on chest X-ray in later life is a common legacy.
recoveryTimeline: In viral pleurisy, the sharp chest pain typically begins to ease within 3-7 days of starting NSAIDs, with most patients feeling substantially better within 1-2 weeks. Return to normal activity usually occurs by 2-4 weeks. Parapneumonic pleurisy follows the course of the underlying pneumonia: fever settles in 2-5 days of appropriate antibiotics and pain resolves over 1-3 weeks. Empyema requiring drainage and surgery may take 3-6 weeks for full recovery. PE-related pleuritic pain often improves within 1-2 weeks of anticoagulation as the embolus is remodelled.
complications: Pleural effusion — accumulation of excess fluid between the pleural layers, causing breathlessness if large; requires drainage when it impairs breathing or when fluid analysis is needed to identify the cause.
Empyema — pus in the pleural space, most often from parapneumonic progression; requires intercostal drain placement and antibiotics, and sometimes surgery to clear loculations.
Fibrothorax — permanent fibrous thickening and sometimes calcification of the pleura in inadequately treated empyema or haemothorax, which traps the lung and reduces breathing capacity.
Recurrent pleurisy — affects approximately 15-30% of patients after a first episode of viral pleurisy; usually managed with a longer course of NSAIDs and, increasingly, colchicine.
Missed pulmonary embolism — the most dangerous diagnostic pitfall; a PE misidentified as benign viral pleurisy goes untreated and carries a risk of fatal recurrence.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pleurisy
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview of pleurisy symptoms, causes, diagnosis, and treatment from NHS England.

supportResources.name: BTS Pleural Disease Guideline
supportResources.description: British Thoracic Society guidelines for clinicians and patients on pleural effusion, empyema, and related conditions.

supportResources.name: Lupus Foundation of America — Lupus and Pleurisy
supportResources.description: Patient education on pleurisy as a manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus.

sources.title: Kass SM, Williams PM, Reamy BV. Pleurisy. American Family Physician, 2007
BTS Pleural Disease Guideline 2023 — British Thoracic Society
Rahman NM et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection (MIST2 trial). NEJM, 2011
Imazio M, Gaita F. Diagnosis and treatment of pericarditis. Heart, 2015
NICE NG115 — Pneumonia in adults: diagnosis and management
Hooper C et al. BTS Pleural Effusion Guidelines. Thorax, 2010
Konstantinides SV et al. ESC Guidelines on Pulmonary Embolism (2019)

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia causes pleuritic chest pain only when infection spreads to the adjacent pleura; pneumonia additionally produces productive cough, high fever, and consolidation on chest X-ray, whereas isolated viral pleurisy causes sharp pain without lung consolidation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Heart failure can cause bilateral pleural effusions with breathlessness and dull chest discomfort, but the sharp inspiratory pain of pleurisy is typically absent; heart failure effusions are transudates, while pleuritis effusions are exudates.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atrial fibrillation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute pericarditis — which is closely related to pleurisy and sometimes occurs together — can trigger atrial fibrillation; pleurisy itself does not, and the distinction is important for management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes chest tightness and wheeze that worsen with breathing but the pain is bilateral and diffuse rather than sharp and one-sided; pleurisy produces a lateralised stabbing pain that is point-specific and friction-rub positive.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Bacterial pneumonia is the leading infectious cause of exudative pleurisy and empyema, making these two conditions frequently intertwined in clinical practice.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Tuberculous pleuritis is among the most common causes of unexplained exudative pleural effusion globally and requires full anti-TB treatment.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: COPD patients are at higher risk of respiratory infections that lead to parapneumonic pleurisy, and their reduced lung reserve makes effusions more symptomatic.

relatedConditions.name: Heart failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Heart failure causes pleural effusions through elevated venous pressure; these are transudates and do not cause pleuritic pain but are part of the differential diagnosis for breathlessness with pleural fluid.

faqs.question: What does pleurisy feel like?
faqs.answer: Pleurisy produces a sharp or stabbing pain on one side of the chest that becomes suddenly worse with every breath in, cough, or movement. Many people describe it as a knife-like catch. The pain eases when they hold the breath or press firmly on the painful area. Some also feel a dull ache in the shoulder or neck on the same side.

faqs.question: What causes pleurisy?
faqs.answer: The most common cause in healthy adults is a viral respiratory infection. Other causes include bacterial pneumonia spreading to the pleura, pulmonary embolism (a blood clot in the lung), autoimmune diseases such as lupus or rheumatoid arthritis, tuberculosis, and rarely cancer involving the pleural surface. Identifying the cause determines the treatment.

faqs.question: Is pleurisy dangerous?
faqs.answer: Viral pleurisy in an otherwise healthy person is not dangerous and resolves within 1-2 weeks. The concern is that pleuritic chest pain can be the presenting symptom of a pulmonary embolism, which is life-threatening and requires anticoagulation. Any sudden, severe pleuritic chest pain — especially with breathlessness, rapid heart rate, or a recent period of immobility — warrants urgent assessment to exclude a blood clot.

faqs.question: How is pleurisy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a history and physical examination; a pleural friction rub heard through a stethoscope is highly characteristic. A chest X-ray checks for effusion or pneumonia. When pulmonary embolism is suspected, a D-dimer blood test and CT pulmonary angiography are performed. If fluid has collected, pleural ultrasound and a sample of the fluid help identify the cause.

faqs.question: How is pleurisy treated?
faqs.answer: For viral pleurisy, NSAIDs such as ibuprofen or indomethacin relieve both pain and inflammation and are taken for 1-4 weeks. Colchicine is added for recurrent episodes. The underlying cause must also be treated: antibiotics for bacterial infection, anticoagulants for pulmonary embolism, steroids and immunosuppressants for autoimmune disease, and anti-TB drugs for tuberculosis.

faqs.question: How long does pleurisy last?
faqs.answer: Viral pleurisy typically resolves within 1-2 weeks with NSAIDs. Some cases take 3-4 weeks to fully clear. About 15-30% of people have a recurrence, usually milder than the first episode. Pleurisy from pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, or autoimmune disease resolves according to the course of the underlying condition — treatment can take weeks to months.

faqs.question: Can pleurisy go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild viral pleurisy may settle without specific treatment over 2-4 weeks, but taking NSAIDs speeds recovery and reduces discomfort substantially. More importantly, pleurisy should not be assumed to be benign without assessment, because dangerous causes like pulmonary embolism need treatment. If pain persists beyond one week or worsens, see a clinician.

faqs.question: Does pleurisy cause shortness of breath?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pleurisy causes breathlessness in two ways: the pain leads people to take deliberately shallow breaths, which impairs lung expansion; and if a pleural effusion develops, the fluid occupies space in the chest and compresses the lung. Large effusions can cause significant breathlessness that requires drainage.

faqs.question: Is pleurisy the same as a pleural effusion?
faqs.answer: No — pleurisy is inflammation of the pleural membranes causing pain, while a pleural effusion is a collection of excess fluid between the layers. They often occur together: inflamed pleura can produce fluid, and the effusion can, paradoxically, reduce pain once it separates the inflamed surfaces. An effusion is investigated and sometimes drained separately from treating the pleuritis itself.

faqs.question: Can stress or anxiety cause pleurisy?
faqs.answer: No. Pleurisy is caused by physical inflammation of the pleural membrane, not by stress or anxiety. However, anxiety can cause chest tightness and pain that is sometimes confused with pleurisy. The key difference is that pleuritic pain is distinctly sharper and clearly worsens with each breath, whereas anxiety chest pain tends to be diffuse, constant, and not timed with respiration.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with pleurisy?
faqs.answer: Vigorous exercise should be avoided during the acute phase while sharp chest pain with breathing is present, as it increases chest wall movement and worsens pain. Gentle walking is fine if tolerable. Once sharp pain has resolved — usually within 1-2 weeks — deep breathing exercises are helpful to re-expand the lung, and normal activity can gradually resume over the following weeks.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pleurisy and pericarditis?
faqs.answer: Pleurisy is inflammation of the pleural lining around the lungs, causing lateralised sharp pain that worsens with breathing. Pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardial sac around the heart, causing central chest pain that is often relieved by leaning forward. Both can occur together. A key distinguishing sign is that pericardial pain lessens when sitting forward, while pleuritic pain improves with breath-holding.

faqs.question: Can pleurisy be a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pleuritic chest pain is sometimes the first symptom of pulmonary embolism, lung cancer, mesothelioma, active tuberculosis, or a flare of lupus. For this reason, unexplained pleurisy — especially if there is no recent viral illness to explain it, or if it does not resolve promptly — warrants investigation beyond treating the pain alone.

faqs.question: What is empyema and how does it relate to pleurisy?
faqs.answer: Empyema is a collection of pus in the pleural space, which develops when bacteria from pneumonia infect the pleural fluid. It is a serious complication of parapneumonic pleurisy characterised by fever, increasing breathlessness, and failure to improve with antibiotics. It requires drainage — usually via a chest tube — and sometimes surgery to clear thickened, infected fluid.

faqs.question: Can lupus cause pleurisy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pleuritis is one of the official diagnostic criteria for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and occurs in up to 60% of patients at some point. Lupus pleuritis causes bilateral or unilateral pleuritic pain and may be accompanied by a small effusion. Treatment involves NSAIDs for pain, corticosteroids for moderate flares, and long-term immunosuppression to prevent recurrences.

faqs.question: What is a pleural friction rub?
faqs.answer: A pleural friction rub is a creaking, leathery, or crunchy sound heard through a stethoscope when inflamed pleural surfaces rub against each other during breathing. It is heard best at the end of inspiration and the beginning of expiration, at the lung base. The rub disappears once fluid accumulates and separates the inflamed surfaces. It is the classic examination finding of pleurisy.

faqs.question: Does pleurisy leave any permanent damage?
faqs.answer: Most acute viral pleurisy resolves without any lasting damage to the pleura or lung. Empyema and tuberculous pleurisy can cause fibrothorax — permanent fibrous thickening or calcification of the pleural layers — visible on imaging in later life, which occasionally reduces lung capacity. Modern prompt treatment of empyema and TB dramatically reduces this risk.

faqs.question: How can I tell if my pleurisy is getting worse?
faqs.answer: Signs of worsening include increasing breathlessness that suggests a growing pleural effusion, a rising fever above 38.5°C that suggests developing empyema or pneumonia, severe pain uncontrolled by NSAIDs, or any new confusion, rapid heart rate, or low blood pressure. These signs need same-day medical assessment. Improving pleurisy means pain gradually reduces day by day.

faqs.question: Can pleurisy cause a blood clot?
faqs.answer: Pleurisy does not cause blood clots. The reverse is more clinically important: a pulmonary embolism (blood clot in the lung) is a common cause of pleuritic chest pain through lung infarction. Always tell your doctor about any risk factors for blood clots — recent surgery, prolonged bed rest, pregnancy, or a history of clots — when being assessed for pleuritic chest pain.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine to prevent pleurisy?
faqs.answer: There is no vaccine specifically for pleurisy. However, the influenza vaccine reduces the risk of viral pleurisy following influenza, and the pneumococcal vaccine reduces bacterial pneumonia that can progress to parapneumonic pleurisy. Annual flu vaccination is recommended for everyone, and pneumococcal vaccination is recommended for adults over 65 and those with chronic disease.

faqs.question: What pain relief is best for pleurisy?
faqs.answer: NSAIDs such as ibuprofen (400-600 mg three times daily) or indomethacin (25-50 mg three times daily) are the most effective for pleuritic pain because they reduce both inflammation and pain. Paracetamol can be added for additional relief. Take NSAIDs with food. Opioids are avoided unless pain is severe because they suppress breathing and the cough reflex.

metaDescription: Pleurisy causes sharp one-sided chest pain that worsens with each breath. Learn causes, diagnosis, treatment and when it signals a blood clot. Doctor-reviewed.
